Summary: FOXP3 is the lineage-determining transcription factor for regulatory T cells. FOXP3 forms a large molecular complex with other transcription factors and enzymatic subunits to regulate regulatory T cell development and function. Tumour-infiltrating FOXP3+ regulatory T cells have strong immunosuppressive function and may promote immune escape and tumour development. Tumour-derived factors may modulate FOXP3+ regulatory T cell induction, migration, and immunosuppressive function. In human cancers, antiangiogenic therapy blocking VEGFR is associated with reduced tumour‑infiltrating FOXP3+ regulatory T cells. VEGF can promote migration of FOXP3+ regulatory T cells and can enhance their immunosuppressive function. The mechanisms linking VEGFR blockade to reduced tumour‑infiltrating FOXP3+ regulatory T cells remain unclear. In colorectal cancer, classical FOXP3+ regulatory T cells are anti-inflammatory and suppress immune cells. This suppression is linked to poorer prognosis. Tissue-resident FOXP3+ subsets in colorectal cancer can be pro-inflammatory. FOXP3(lo) non-suppressive T cells lack CD45RA, show FOXP3 instability, and secrete IFN-γ. Tumour production of IL-12, TGF-β, and TNF-α induced by Fusobacterium nucleatum alters Treg heterogeneity and facilitates expansion of pro-inflammatory FOXP3(lo) non‑Treg cells. Expansion of FOXP3(lo) non‑Treg cells enhances anti-tumour immunity and correlates with better patient survival.
References: Qiu, Y., Ke, S., Chen, J., Qin, Z., Zhang, W., Yuan, Y., Meng, D., Zhao, G., Wu, K., Li, B., & Li, D. (2022). FOXP3+ regulatory T cells and the immune escape in solid tumours. Front Immunol, 13, 982986. https://doi.org/10.3389/fimmu.2022.982986
